steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers concluded that sample F07 formed higher in the water column than M24, based purely on the oxygen-18 concentrations matching different depths (135 m vs 370 m). For that conclusion to hold, the oxygen-18 level must reflect depth only — not sex. A finding that directly eliminates sex as a confounding variable would lock in the depth interpretation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Among nautilids, there is no effect of sex on the uptake of oxygen-18 from water or its deposition in septa.: This removes sex as an alternative explanation, so the isotope difference can only mean a depth difference. That directly supports the conclusion. ✓
  • Choice B — Male nautilids tend to show higher concentrations of oxygen-18 than do female nautilids inhabiting the same point in the water column.: This introduces a sex effect on oxygen-18, which would actually undermine the conclusion by offering an alternative cause for the difference. ✗
  • Choice C — In nautilids, sex predicts septa growth rate more strongly than does oxygen-18 concentration in the surrounding water.: Growth rate is irrelevant to whether depth or sex explains the oxygen-18 data. ✗
  • Choice D — Regardless of sex, nautilids spend most of their lives at depths of 50 meters or greater, where oxygen-18 concentrations typically differ from those higher in the water column.: This gives a general depth habit but doesn't address whether sex could independently affect isotope levels, so it doesn't directly support the conclusion. ✗
That is the correct answer.
